knowledge abstraction layers 

when do you know you know something? 

how deep does one have to go to say that you understand “this”?

i could understand a concept on surface with probably some analogies 

but i’m not satisfied with my understanding so i tend to go 1-2 or sometimes 3 layers of abstraction deep to get an intuitive and solid understanding

“being satisfied” is subjective hence it’s going to vary from person to person 

so when a person A says he knows something how do you judge? each layer of understanding is knowledge 

idk, i’m struggling with this since high school

anyone who has used GPT to code anything that’s seemingly more complex (slightly) knows that -  at the moment you can mostly  get a rough outline of the code and you’ll still have to sit and fix it’s errors and replace some things https://t.co/aJ3RXuG27s

@yishan @krishnanrohit i might be wrong, but i remember David Deutsch saying something along the same lines on the recent Tim Ferriss podcast 

pretty interesting way to think about it, though i don’t know how well the metaphor holds up
@krishnanrohit @yishan @ESYudkowsky thoughts?

@krishnanrohit @yishan @ESYudkowsky okay, i have got a couple of thoughts (mostly naive):

idk how we could solve the “alignment” problem when it’s so poorly defined and even when it's explained it’s a very high-level abstraction (at least to me and i could convince myself but i don’t fully believe it)

@krishnanrohit @yishan @ESYudkowsky preemptively solving an ill-defined problem with high levels of abstraction that is too hard to comprehend or define with rigorous theory doesn’t seem like a good idea

many are purporting to “pull the plug” because we’re treating this as to be a certainty…

@krishnanrohit @yishan @ESYudkowsky …when it’s merely probabilistic. if we know with high confidence that alignment issues are not going to be a step function but rather a relatively slow/observable/detectable problem - could be like a controlled fire drill.

@krishnanrohit @yishan @ESYudkowsky then, whilst we iterate and improve the existing systems, could we not just use see what’s causing it to go berserk and then develop a better definition of the alignment issue and try solving it? 

i could be ignorant here - many have this perception that this is a “black box”…

@krishnanrohit @yishan @ESYudkowsky i’d argue it’s mostly a gray box - you mostly know what’s happening if you have taken any optimization class. 

my naive speculation is that with GPT-4-like tools making learning easier than ever, i’d expect people to get a pretty decent understanding of the existing system.

@krishnanrohit @yishan @ESYudkowsky the issue is - how do you incentivize a layman to learn, understand and solve (impending) issues.

most people outside of this field don’t even understand how big of a deal this is - they don’t even acknowledge its existence and many are dismissive.

@krishnanrohit i’d say that, being worried of one’s self-worth or having existential crisis is just a knee-jerk reaction. my first reaction to it back in Nov. was very similar and it was demoralizing particularly when i got it to do 2 weeks of code writing in less than a day.

@krishnanrohit now i’ve been using it almost on a daily basis to learn and do things i wouldn’t previously even dared to do just because i was intimidated by some abstract math filled with esoteric theories. 

if anyone is using it to learn, soon you can’t imagine going a day without having it

deformable object manipulation is one of the hard parts of robotic manipulation. when you’re dealing with infinite-dimensional states you cannot model it as state, action pair and hoping to predict the shape with neural networks given a particular action is not the best approach https://t.co/1YS8BnyeTs

imo teleop is a stop-gap measure and it certainly works for complex manipulations but like everything else, it fails at dealing with out of distribution tasks. if you need it to be completely autonomous, you need to close the loop with extremely good real-time sensor feedback

there’re are some decent papers on deformable object manipulation that does not use demonstrations alas the success rate is not great. far fetched: build a perception system which is capable of discerning the material’s physical properties which can then be used to model https://t.co/jMw87KMu9p

something that’s under-discussed is occlusion: this is an even bigger problem with deformable objects, when you’re working with wires that overlap, your perception modules struggle to segment and identify when everything has the same color with non-existent background contrast

there are some interesting papers that don’t use any nn and are able to predict the motion of wires/ropes in real-time even under occlusion, the only downside is it's not fast enough even when everything in python is ported to c++ https://t.co/2Rq3LRbIB7

robotics field struggles the most because building hardware-agnostic software is currently not possible and anyone who claims otherwise would’ve a fine print of caveats.
imagine a world where we had 10s of thousands of cpu architectures where each required its own instruction set
instead of having one or two dominating architectures which has made everyone’s life easy.

i believe with the current paradigm and Tesla’s resources, they might have a upper hand in scaling their humanoids which definitely makes it more plausible for being the most general-purpose bot in the market.
BUT scaling robots and scaling llms with data are not at all in the same timescales, so i don’t expect things to change overnight and t’ll be incremental if the trends continue.

one thing that can alleviate the exhaustive human resources necessary for methods that use demonstrations is by using something like NVIDIA’s Omniverse platform, their simulators are getting really good and if compute is not an issue, this is an excellent alternative
